sentences.text: No, they never did.
They were taken to Auschwitz.
Correct.
Well, from the time that this family took me in till the Russians liberated Nyiregyhaza, there were-- almost every night there were bombings.
And because of the fact that I was hiding and they were afraid to be caught, every night when the bomb airplanes came, they went to a shelter and I was left in a bed under covers alone.
And that-- that is the most horrible part of the-- my life that I can remember, being alone in the dark and the bombings night after night after night and I was all alone.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
A year, a little over a year.
And liberation.
And not directly, but after a while, first he-- it's complicated.
But eventually, we ended up in his home where he was hiding.
The man and the woman didn't agree on having me there.
So it wasn't a pleasant-- it was a very unpleasant situation.
They made me work very hard.
They treated me like I was a servant and the children were very unkind to me.
So when my uncle came home, it was a wonderful surprise.
But he died after three weeks being home.
Yes, they would.
Unfortunately, when I was a little girl, my-- my mother sent in a picture to a-- a coffee commercial and we won the prize.
And there where big signs all over Hungary of drink coffee and my face was on it as a child.
And everyone knew it was a-- a Jewish child.
So I had a familiar face, which didn't make life easy for these people that were hiding me.
Because it was just an immediate-- immediately, they would know who I was.
Nothing.
Yes.
No.
I wasn't allowed to do anything.
I laid in bed.
Once in a while, at night, they let me go out into the courtyard late.
Once in a while, the grandmother let me help her cook.
But basically, I don't remember.
I was just-- I did nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No, no, nothing at all.
I-- I cried for my mother for a long time.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember anything about that at all.
That happened in the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was trying to tell you, my-- I remember him as a very elegant man.
He did things differently than most people I ever met.
And he took a little crystal glass with the liquor and toasted.
And he had said, I will not come back, but I want you to carry on and do things nicely, make things beautiful.
And after that, we never spoke about it again.
He told me that my mother doesn't want me to leave and I-- I-- I-- I-- if I remember now, it would be he defied my mother by sending me away.
Consequently, they both went to Auschwitz.
My mother did survive because of the sisters that had been with her, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who together they kind of took care of each other.
My father was killed immediately, very, very, very soon.
I think it-- he was taken in end of May.
Beginning of June, they got to Auschwitz and by August, he was dead.
His stomach, he had a very bad ulcer.
He was taken to Mauthausen.
A friend came back to Hungary and gave me dates, gave me circumstances of how he was killed.
Yes.
The friend's name?
No.
In camp?
Yes, as a matter of fact, quite a bit.
They-- my mother was the biggest of them and the oldest.
She-- she was-- she almost didn't make it, but as I said before, they hurt them.
They were beaten, they were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they-- they did not know what was going on.
They really did not know about the gas chambers around them.
They-- they did know, however, that had I been with my mother, they would have never been able to be together.
I mean, my mother did survive because I wasn't there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My other aunt, who also had a child my age, the child never came back.
When my mother and my aunts came back from the camp, I remember them coming into town.
There were all wearing red blouses because they had red flags.
The Russians liberated them and from the red flags they were-- they made this.
So the four ladies arrived in the red.
Made from Communist flags.
Made some Communist flags.
And gray flannel slacks made from blankets.
And they-- they said-- they told some bad stories about what happened.
My mother didn't know too much, because she-- she was really in a working camp.
My-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mother didn't know that my father passed away until-- until we-- until she came home.
Immy.
And he took me back home to my house where we used to live with my parents.
And the Gestapo headquarters used to be there.
So everything was destroyed.
Was in my house, yes.
Yes.
Right.
Headquarters.
Right.
So when I got back there with my uncle, there was nothing left.
And everything was destroyed, everything was taken.
And he hired someone to take care of me, and went to Budapest to find my parents.
He had no idea where they are.
And while he was looking for them among the dead and so on, he got typhus.
He-- he contracted, and he died.
So once again, I was left alone.
Yes.
The people that were hiding me were-- came in one morning and said that the Russians are coming, and that maybe my parents are coming with them.
I mean, the naivete of the people were amazing.
And there were big street fights.
People were hungry, had no money, no food.
They were eating the horses.
The horses were killed in the town, and that's what we ate.
That's the only food that was around for about three weeks.
There was no food to be had.
Yes.
I have one very fond memory of a Russian soldier, when he came and came into the house and wanted to-- they took, they looted quite a bit, the Russian soldiers.
But this one man started to take some of the things from this family, and they said, well, this is a Jewish child.
So he sat down and he left them everything.
And he kept coming back and bringing candy and food to the family.
And about a week after liberation, he invited all his friends, like 20 Russian soldiers.
We had a big banquet of food, and I was seated at the head of the table as the young Jewish child.
And they danced with me, and music.
It was-- it was amazing.
And there was no communication.
I didn't speak Russian, obviously, but they understood.
I don't know.
I wonder.
He had to be.
Yes.
Josef.
No.
Yes.
I mean, what a-- what a insight this man had, thinking back.
And young, he was very young.
I remember he was very young, a very jolly and happy man.
That my parents are coming home.
But that-- it took a long, long time till my parents finally-- or my mother finally got home.
Months and months and months.
Well, I-- I stayed with a neighbor.
I told you my uncle died.
I got-- I stayed in-- I was in my house, and the neighbor took me.
Then an aunt came from-- my father's sister came, who is now living in Israel.
Her is [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] .
Schonfeld.
But right now, I mean, it's-- her name is different now, but that was my father's sister, younger sister.
They were--
Right now?
Yes.
Oh my god.
Neaman.
N-E-A-M-A-N.
Israel.
And her husband is-- I think was born in Israel.
Doctor Neaman.
Emil Neaman.
She's my favorite aunt.
Oh, I shouldn't say that on tape.
She's lovely.
She took care of me.
Yes.
Exactly.
Nine years old.
Yes.
And the-- the-- the most interesting part of the liberation to me is I was the only child left in Nyiregyhaza.
Out of all the Jewish children, not one came back, except me.
No.
Nobody returned.
No one.
No, it's not as far as I know.
It's a fact.
Because every mother and father that came back after the war used to come to our home, after my mother and sisters and all came back, just to touch me, just to-- I was the most popular kid in town.
No.
There was--
No child.
No.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Yes, my husband, Stuart, who I met in 1975, was very interested in my story.
And I realized that, up until then, I never spoke about it.
And when-- he really wanted me to go back with him and show him where I was born.
So we went back.
We went to Budapest, and that was a nice surprise.
Budapest is a beautiful city.
And then we drove to Nyiregyhaza.
And I remembered, I had a picture of the streets, my home.
I was very proud of this house, because I remembered it to be a palace.
It's not a palace, but I remembered it being one.
And we drove into town, and it was so much bigger.
They built a lot of different streets.
The streets were paved.
Some of the streets I remember being dirt roads.
And I remembered when my grandmother's house was in grandmother's store.
I remembered everything.
And it was still there.
And we went to my-- my street, and I went to my-- my home.
And I-- three families, three communist families were living in this house right now.
And so we looked around.
And all of a sudden, from nowhere, I started to tell my husband that, you see, this is where the German soldiers came into the house and asked me to leave in the ghetto when they were taking us away.
I wanted a glass of water, and I went to get it.
And he said [SPEAKING GERMAN] And nobody ever told me to get out in my life.
I was a spoiled little girl.
And the harshness, I'll never forget that.
And I started to explain this to my husband, that here I was in this room, and then the Germans soldiers were in this room.
And all of a sudden, all I could see is German soldiers.
So Stuart--
I saw them.
I saw them.
It's the most amazing thing.
And so Stuart got nervous, and he took me out of my home, thinking that once I'm out of the house, it will pass.
So we got back in the car, and we started to drive around in town.
And then I saw the soldiers.
I saw-- and the worst part of it is I saw all the Hungarian Sandor.
Sandor was a policeman on a horse with a big rooster's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] feather in his hat.
And I saw them.
I saw them on every corner, and I saw them-- they-- [SOBBING] [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I saw them.
[PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] I don't know what they did to me, these guys, but every time I think of them-- that's-- this part is tough.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
Yeah.
They were Hungarian Nazis.
And it was easy for them just to get off their horse and beating on people.
Yeah.
Being Jewish is a good cause.
Yes, they were everywhere.
They were everywhere.
You could see them everywhere.
Yes.
Civil rights?
I don't think those were-- existed among the Jews in Hungary.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My father was beaten before he was killed for stealing an apple in concentration camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I don't eat apples.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Yes, we left the town and went back to Budapest, and everything was fine the next day.
It was-- it was a very strange experience.
How did I--
My mother remarried, and my stepfather and I were brought here by my uncle.
Uncle Micky, yes.
Right.
He brought us out.
He brought my aunts, my-- myself, my mother, and stepfather.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came to America in 1951, November 30th.
And just about this Christmas, I already was working.
You know Jay Thorpe?
Jay Thorpe.
Department stores.
You wouldn't remember.
You're too young.
It was on 57th Street, and I didn't speak a word of English.
And I started to work as a junior model.
On the floor, I was modeling, because you didn't need to speak English.
And then 1955, I got married.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1956, I had my first child.
His name is Kenneth, who is now a doctor. 1958, I had my daughter, Cherie, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who's married and has two children.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
Yeah, Well, I think that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in America, the belief that it can't happen is a very scary notion.
I think being married to an American and having American children, I understand how they don't understand that it can happen again.
And it's very important that we do what we do just now.
I don't enjoy doing what I'm doing, telling you my life.
It's very private, and it hurts me to tell you, because I don't want anyone to know.
But I'm doing it because I think this is important.
And if everyone will pitch in and do what I'm doing, and-- and-- and my children will do it and your children will do it, maybe-- maybe they won't hate us so much.
That is my mother, Lily Bohm, before she was taken to Auschwitz.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is my father, Laszlo Schonfeld, about five years before the war.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Yes.
This was one of the pictures that survived.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mother and I on vacation in a seaside town, I think.
It-- I-- it looks like I'm two years old there, maybe less.
That is a friend.
I don't know who he is.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is Uncle Bela Bohm, who survived the war but died later in a drowning accident.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Those are my two cousins that are now living in Israel and I in the Czechoslovakian Tatra.
It's right after the war.
I'm waiting for my mother to come back from Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is I in Munchen, probably in 1950, thinking I'm very beautiful, I think.
I must have given it to a boyfriend.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the that brought my stepfather, my mother, and I to America.
It wasn't a luxury cruiser.
We came into New York.
We left Bremerhaven and came into New York.
It was a horrendous trip.
It was in November.
Unbelievable.
That is an incredible-- you can't imagine that feeling.
That-- that's-- that's unbelievable.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Kenneth and Cherie at Ken's bar mitzvah.
And that's my Lhasa Apso, Tutu.
Tutu is no longer with us.
My daughter was born in '58, and Kenneth was born in '56.
Don't ask me how old they are, because I never remember their ages.
I choose not to.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those are my grandchildren, Jade and Bo.
It's a recent picture, about a year old.
They live in Florida, and they're wonderful children.
I love them very much.
Bo--
Bo is 11, and Jade is 4.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That is Stuart my husband, and I think that's me.
